title: How to use AI – and keep staff levels up
full_text:
Zeller, a payments platform worth more than $1bn, has used artificial intelligence to crush its product development cycle from nine months to under four weeks, shipping more than 10 new features every 30 days.
But co-founder and chief executive Ben Pfisterer says AI is not coming for jobs, replacing software engineers and product developers, because it’s a tool that makes those experts more flexible at responding to customers’ needs.
Mr Pfisterer said Zeller slashed its product development cycle by doing the opposite of almost everyone else – refusing to hand staff a blanket order to use AI.
He said such mandates lead to runaway bills bleeding bigger players dry. Uber burned through its annual Anthropic budget in four months, and has since topped it as AI becomes the first technology that risks becoming more expensive to run than human workers.
“A lot of those overspends is because pretty much everyone is getting the mandate you must use it, so you’re kind of going in where everyone is going through exactly the same learning curve, and they start incredibly broad,” he said.
“While the medium and long-term benefits are huge, it can be a huge distraction as everyone is trying to learn without showing each other. So we’ve tried to avoid that.” Mr Pfisterer – who has criticised the Victorian government’s attempt to legislate a right to work from home at least two days a week – has instead seeded the technology through a handful of team leaders before pushing it outwards.
“Certain people in our teams have been like the leaders in that space, and they’ve actually adopted and started testing it, and then bringing everyone else along in their team,” he said.
“And then those teams bring other teams along, so rather than everyone just trying to shotgun approach, trying to learn it and deploy, we’ve been a lot more targeted.” The system has enabled Zeller to launch a point-of-sale system that takes on global incumbents such as Square, Shopify and Lightspeed.
“It’s now positioning us as the first truly end-to-end financial services business in Australia – all the way from your stockings, your inventory, taking the payment to reconciling and settlement, and then managing your funds in your accounts, and using your cards, and making payments to staff, etc. That’s a full end-to-end solution,” Mr Pfisterer said.
Zeller estimates the average Australian hospitality or retail business spends up to $17,000 a year on point-of-sale software, hardware and payment infrastructure.
Zeller POS strips out the subscription fee and charges a flat rate per transaction when paired with its terminal.
An automatic import tool cuts set-up for switching merchants from days to minutes.
The product was tested with local customers over the past two months and became available to the fintech’s 110,000-plus Australian merchants this week.
Mr Pfisterer said the company had been able to use AI to respond to Zellers customers more quickly and create products that better suit their needs, with software engineers and product developers still playing a crucial role. This is despite companies such as WiseTech Global and Afterpay owner Block sacking thousands of staff and replacing them with AI.
“I see absolutely every bit of feedback,” Mr Pfisterer said. “We see the most requested ask, and then we get to build it. “The sort of irony of what’s happening with engineering at the moment is that software was developing quicker and quicker, engineers had to do less of the QA (quality assurance) side.
“Now it’s actually reverting the other way, where the AI is obviously building, like taking a lot of the actual coding, and then for really good engineers make sure that the guardrails are on, that’s being built the way we want to build it.” But this isn’t creating a workforce of so-called “bot-sitters”. “What we’re finding is designers who had more of an engineering bent can now go further in that value chain,” Mr Pfisterer said.
“Designers that have more of a testing or user interface and a customer experience bent can now go into that space. So, rather than limiting your job opportunities or restricting it, it’s enhancing and giving you more capability to do the broader set of it.” AI has lifted feature delivery by up to 70 per cent, Mr Pfisterer said, with the next batch of functionality now going out every one to two weeks.
Anthropic’s Claude is Zeller’s primary model. Google’s Gemini does more of the business work, and ChatGPT handles strategy.
Mr Pfisterer said this had given Zeller an edge over the big banks. While the banks have been big adopters of AI, they have been slow to develop new products with the technology, he said.
“They’re sticking to the same products they’ve always had,” he said. “They aren’t iterating, they aren’t innovating, and they’re just resting on their laurels.” Zeller opened its first office in the UK in April. Mr Pfisterer said the UK expansion was running ahead of schedule, and Europe is next.
